coredata.dc:title: Design of an efficient fluorescent nanoplatform carrier for hydrophobic drugs along with green carbon dot: Possible application in cancer image-guided drug therapy
coredata.dc:creator: Hashemi, Fatemeh
Mohajeri, Nasrin
Radnia, Fatemeh
Zarghami, Nosratollah
coredata.dc:description: Nanotechnology has taken novel approaches to advance cancer therapeutic efficacy employing multifunctional nanocarriers with drug delivery and imaging function. In this work, we designed a biocompatible and affordable fluorescence nanocarrier called chitosan (CS)-carbon dot (CD) hybrid nanogel for cell imaging. The green CDs were synthesized using tomato juice through a simple single-step hydrothermal method. Chitosan polymer was used as a carrier for co-delivery CDs and the anti-cancer drug with low solubility, silibinin (Sil), to design the Sil-chitosan carbon dots hybrid nanogels (CCHNs) system. After optimizing the physicochemical properties of nanostructure by DLS, FTIR, FESEM, TEM, and UV-visible techniques, the successful uptake of the fluorescent nanoparticle conjugates into MCF-7 breast cancer cells occurred. Then we embedded CDs in chitosan nanogel. The resultant CCHNs demonstrated optical properties similar to free-CDs, a desirable size distribution (55.22 nm) with a positive surface charge, a suitable loading capacity for Sil (35%), and drug release vulnerable to pH changes. The fluorescent nanocarrier could transfer Sil to MCF-7 cancer cells without remarkable toxicity. The results of the fluorescent microscope indicated that after 4 h, the solid fluorescent signal was received from cells containing CCHNs compared to free CDs and confirmed the ability of hybrid nanogels to high cellular uptake. This study demonstrates a multifunctional nanocarrier containing therapeutic compounds and fluorescent agents that provide cellular imaging to enhance therapeutic efficacy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bioimaging
Chitosan
Carbon dot
Hybrid nanogel
Silibinin
Nanocarrier
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of chitosan-carbon dots hybrid nanogels (CCHNs)
In vitro Sil loading and release from CCHNs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM overview image of CCHNs (a), FESEM image of aggregated CCHNs (b).

(a) UV-Vis absorption spectrum of CS nanogel (a), CCHNs (b), and free CDs (c), inset shows photograph of CCHNs under UV light at 360 nm (d). (b) PL spectra of CCHNs were recorded at excitation wavelengths 340 to 440 nm. (c) FTIR spectrum of the CS nanogel (a), free CD (b), and CCHNs (c).
In vitro drug release. Sil was releasing kinetics from CCHNs in PBS solution (pH = 7.4 and 5) at 37 °C.
Fluorescence microscopy images of MCF-7 cells treated with free CD and CCHNs after 1 and 4 h.

Cell viability of MCF-7 cells against CCHNs (a), Sil, and Sil-CCHNs with various concentration for 24 h (b), and 48 h (c). *p < 0.05 versus the MCF-7 cells incubated with Sil-CCHNs. #p < 0.05 versus the MCF-7 cells incubated with Sil.
Summary of parameters obtained for characterization of the synthesized nanoparticles.

head.ce:title: Design of an efficient fluorescent nanoplatform carrier for hydrophobic drugs along with green carbon dot: Possible application in cancer image-guided drug therapy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hematology and Oncology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aHematology and Oncology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Clinical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bDepartment of Clinical Biochemistry and Laboratory Medicine, Faculty of Medicine, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cDepartment of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Medical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Istanbul Aydin University, Istanbul, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dDepartment of Medical Biochemistry, Faculty of Medicine, Istanbul Aydin University, Istanbul, Turkey

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Medical Biotechnology, Faculty of Advanced Medical Sciences, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanotechnology has taken novel approaches to advance cancer therapeutic efficacy employing multifunctional nanocarriers with drug delivery and imaging function. In this work, we designed a biocompatible and affordable fluorescence nanocarrier called chitosan (CS)-carbon dot (CD) hybrid nanogel for cell imaging. The green CDs were synthesized using tomato juice through a simple single-step hydrothermal method. Chitosan polymer was used as a carrier for co-delivery CDs and the anti-cancer drug with low solubility, silibinin (Sil), to design the Sil-chitosan carbon dots hybrid nanogels (CCHNs) system. After optimizing the physicochemical properties of nanostructure by DLS, FTIR, FESEM, TEM, and UV-visible techniques, the successful uptake of the fluorescent nanoparticle conjugates into MCF-7 breast cancer cells occurred. Then we embedded CDs in chitosan nanogel. The resultant CCHNs demonstrated optical properties similar to free-CDs, a desirable size distribution (55.22 nm) with a positive surface charge, a suitable loading capacity for Sil (35%), and drug release vulnerable to pH changes. The fluorescent nanocarrier could transfer Sil to MCF-7 cancer cells without remarkable toxicity. The results of the fluorescent microscope indicated that after 4 h, the solid fluorescent signal was received from cells containing CCHNs compared to free CDs and confirmed the ability of hybrid nanogels to high cellular uptake. This study demonstrates a multifunctional nanocarrier containing therapeutic compounds and fluorescent agents that provide cellular imaging to enhance therapeutic efficacy.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite recent advances , cancer is a significant threat to human health [1]. Chemotherapy can result in a destructive impact on normal cells leading to side effects on cancer patients. With the help of nanotechnology sciences more targeted treatment may be possible allowing for diminished side effects.. Therefore, designing nano-structures to attain a traceable and targeted drug delivery to increase therapeutic efficiency and imaging function is of importance [2,3]. Recently, fluorescence imaging via nanomaterials, including quantum dots, luminescent metal complexes, carbon dots, and nitrogen doped carbon dots has gained significant attention for cell imaging, metal detection, drug delivery, and other biomedical applications [3,4]. Carbon dots can play a key role in chemiluminescence reaction as catalysts, energy acceptors, and redox reactions providing various reagents and biological molecules detection [5,6]. Among different contrast agents, the advantages of carbon dots over chemical fluorescent dyes and quantum dots can be listed as low cytotoxicity, property stability, eco-friendliness, high photoluminescence, excellent biocompatibility, and solubility with no modification of the surface chemistry. These properties have made them suitable as optical probes for bioimaging applications. Furthermore, it is believed that the excitation-dependent optical properties of carbon dots are related to the functional groups (including -OH, -COOH, -NH2) and surface energy trap [7–9]. Recently, many efforts have been allocated to the development of green synthesis methods of fluorescent carbon dot using natural precursors [10]. Hence, natural precursors such as egg [11], potato [12,13], coffee [14], and the extract of all kinds of fruits [15] have attracted much more attention as raw materials due to their availability, being eco-friendly, low costs, and non-toxicity [10]. Among various methods for carbon dot synthesis [4], the hydrothermal method does not require expensive equipment and tedious experimental procedures. It has gained significant attention as an appropriate method for producing of green carbon dots [16].

For instance, some herbal components have been efficient in preventing cancer due to their antioxidant properties. One of these natural anticancer agents is Sil, extracted from the silybum marianum and is a polyphenolic flavonolignan. This antioxidant compound can affect different mechanisms including cell cycle regulation, metastasis, invasion, and apoptosis induction [17]. Although this anticancer drug is used to treat various cancers such as breast, skin, liver, and prostate, the low aqueous solubility of Sil due to hydrophobic and multi-ring structure has reduced their clinical application [17,18]. In addressing this issue, nanocarriers have been recognized as promising cancer treatment approaches by improving drug dissolution rate, decreasing drug dosage, and reducing the side effects on normal cells [17,19]. To develop the therapeutic efficacy, the co-delivery of fluorescent and therapeutic agents would be highly desirable due to the ability to monitor the progress of treatment by identifying the accurate location of the cancerous cells. On the other hand, the combination of carbon dots with the anticancer drug provides a multifunctional system to transfer traceable drugs [20,21]. In this framework, chitosan nanogel, a suitable biomaterial nanocarrier, shows potential for co-delivery application because of biodegradability, non-toxicity, biocompatibility, and non-immunogenic properties [20]. Chitosan is a polymer composed of β-(1→4)-linked D-glucosamine and N-Acetyl-D-glucosamine produced by deacetylation of chitin. Compared to natural biopolymers, chitosan nanogel possesses many surface functional groups (NH2/OH), making interactions with negatively charged surfaces like the cell membranes increase cellular penetration. In addition, the stimuli-responsive properties of chitosan nanogel are desirable to control drug and carbon dot release to develop a multifunctional system [22–24].

In this paper, we created a new biocompatible system by immobilizing the green carbon dot as a fluorescence agent into the pH-responsive polymer nanogel. The aim was simultaneous bioimaging and pH-responsive drug delivery to cancer cells. Sil, as an anticancer agent, was considered the hydrophobic model drug agent. Finally, it is shown that the two-component hybrid nanogel (chitosan-carbon dot), with collective properties, has preserved each constituent material's characteristics, leading to the successful integration of several significant functions.

Tomatoes were purchased from a local grocery shop (Tabriz, Iran). Chitosan with a low molecular weight (MW: 50–190 KD), Sil (>98%), glutaraldehyde (25%), MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2, 5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide), and RPMI 1640 were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was prepared from Gibco (USA). Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), acetic acid, ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), phosphate-buffered salin (PBS), trypsin, streptomycin, and penicillin were obtained from Merck chemicals co. MCF-7 cells were supplied from the Pasteur Institute Cell Bank (Iran). Deionized (DI) water was used in all experiments. Nanostructure and morphology of the CCHNs were observed by a field emission scanning electron microscope system (FE-SEM, TESCAN MIRA3) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Leo 906 Zeiss Germany). The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of CDs, CCHNs, and CCHNs-Sil were determined by the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique (Zetasier-ZS90, Malvern, UK). The surface functional groups of CDs, CS, and CCHNs were recorded by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Bruker, Germany) at room temperature. Photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra, UV spectra, and cellular imaging of each sample were obtained by cytation5 cell imaging (Multi-Mode Reader-BioTek, USA).

CDs were prepared via a green one-step hydrothermal method from tomato juice through a previously reported method. Firstly 10 ml of tomato juice was poured into heat-resistant bottles and was heated up to 150 °C. After 8 h, the red-brown solution was cooled down to ambient temperature. The resulting CDs were centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 30 min to remove the large particle with less fluorescent intensity. Moreover, the red supernatant solution was filtered through a 0.22 µm filter membrane to separate the desirable products. The filtrate solution was dialyzed in 500 ml deionized water via a dialysis membrane with cut-off 100 MWCO for 48 h. Finally, the purified CDs were dried during lyophilization, and the powdered CDs could be stored at 4 °C for two months in darkness for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of chitosan-carbon dots hybrid nanogels (CCHNs)
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent CCHNs is prepared according to the one-step sedimentation method. Concisely, 25 mg of chitosan powder was dissolved in 2.5 ml of aqueous acetic acid (1%) and stirred for 24 h, at ambient temperature. Then, 10 mg of CD powder was dissolved in 2.5 ml of aqueous acetic acid (1%) and added drop by drop into the chitosan solution and stirred for 60 min at 70 °C while the pH was around 5.0. Then, 3 mg EDTA powder was added. After stirring for 60 min, a few drops of ethanol (non-solvent) was added to the solution. The obtained CCHNs were cross-linked by adding 45 μL GA solution (25%) for 6 h at room temperature. The resultant reaction solution was purified with repeated centrifugation at 20,000 rpm for 30 min. Each time the supernatant was discarded, the precipitate was dialyzed using a dialysis membrane (cutoff: 12,000–14,000). This process was continued for three days to remove free CDs, EDTA, ethanol. Finally, the powder of fluorescent CCHNs was collected by lyophilization and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro Sil loading and release from CCHNs
ce:section.ce:para:
Before loading the Sil into the chitosan-carbon dots hybrid nanogels, a Sil solution was firstly adjusted at pH = 7.4 and a concentration of 5 mg/ml. 0.4 ml of prepared Sil solution was added dropwise to 5 ml of hybrid nanogels (CCHNs) dispersion with 1 mg/ml concentration, and the solution was stirred for 24 h. Afterward, the residual free Sil was separated from Sil-CCHNs suspension by centrifugation at 20,000 rpm for 30 min. The Sil-CCHNs complexes were washed with distilled water four times to remove the free Sil from the hybrid nanogel surface. Finally, to determine the amount of unloaded Sil, the absorbance of the washing and supernatant solutions were measured at a wavelength of 288 nm. The following formula was used to calculate the encapsulation efficiency and Sil-loading content:
Encapsulation
Efficiency
( % )
=
M
t o t a l
d r u g
−
M
d r u g
i n
s u p e r n a t a n t
M
t o t a l
d r u g
× 100 %
Drug
Loading
Content
( % )
=
M
d r u g
i n
h y b r i d
n a n o g e l
M
h y b r i d
n a n o g e l
× 100 %

The release experiment of Sil from Sil-CCHNs in response to the pH changes was studied by the dialysis method. Initially, the Sil-loaded CCHNs were dispersed in 15 ml PBS solution (pH = 7.4, 0.005 M). Next, three dialysis bags, each including 5 ml of the solution mentioned above, were immersed in 50 ml PBS solution (0.005 M) at pH = 5, 6.2, and 7.4, respectively. Finally, the released Sil in PBS solution was sampled at specific time intervals and measured by UV-Vis spectrophotometer at 288 nm.

MCF-7 cancer cells were used to evaluate the efficiency of the resultant chitosan-CDs hybrid nanogels for cellular imaging. MCF-7 cells were cultured in a 6-well plate at a density of 3 × 104 in RPMI 1640 medium and incubated for 24 h at 5% CO2, 37 °C, and 95% humidity. Next, the surface medium was removed, followed by incubating the cells for 1 and 4 h with another medium containing CDs and CS-CD nanogels (equivalent concentration: 100 µg/ml). The cells treated with nanoparticles then washed with PBS two times and fixed in 4% cold paraformaldehyde at 37 °C for 20 min. Finally, to confirm the nanoparticle uptake and fluorescence imaging, cells containing fluorescence nanoparticles were observed by fluorescence microscopy (cytation5 cell imaging Multi-Mode Reader, BIOEK) with an excitation wavelength of 377 nm [25].

The MTT assay was tested to analyze the cytotoxicity of the free form of Sil, Sil-loaded CCHNs, and CCHNs. Briefly, MCF-7 cells at a density of 8 × 103 cells per well with 200 µL medium were cultured into a 96-well plate. They were then incubated for 24 h at 5% CO2, 37 °C, and 95% humidity. After that, the medium was replaced with various concentrations (10, 20, 40, 80, and 160 µg/mL) of fresh medium containing Sil, Sil-CCHNs, and CCHNs and incubated for 24, 48 h. Then, the cells with the addition of MTT solution (10 µL, 5 mg/mL in PBS) were incubated for 4 h. Next, the medium was changed with DMSO (100 µL), followed by shaking for 20 min. Finally, the optical density value of the plate was measured at 570 nm by an ELISA reader.

In this work, the CS-CD hybrid nanogel (CCHN) was created to increase the therapeutic efficacy of the anti-cancer drug as a traceable drug delivery nanocarrier. Chitosan hydrogel delivery can provide sustained release of drug at different spatial conditions. Chitosan hybrid nanogel serves as a platform with physiochemical interactions capability with the encapsulated drugs because of their tunable physical properties and potential to encapsulate the hydrophobic drugs [26]. To prepare the fluorescent and pH-sensitive nanocarriers, firstly, fluorescent CDs were synthesized from a natural carbon source (tomato juice) using a simple and inexpensive green hydrothermal method [27]. Afterward, the biocompatible CCHNs were synthesized through a one-step sedimentation method. The CDs were expected to be trapped into the chitosan chains with hydrogen bonding interactions between the -NH2/-OH groups of chitosan and -COOH/-OH groups of CDs. Finally, to use CCHNs, for the controlled release of therapeutic agents and cell imaging, Sil was loaded into the hybrid nanogel. After characterizing the drug-loaded nanocarriers, the bio-imaging application and the cytotoxicity of the nanocarriers were investigated. CCHN (size 30) with excellent biocompatibility, smart delivery ability, and imaging function have shown a key role in controlling efficient drug delivery. In comparison with traditional drug delivery systems, the advantages of CCHN nanocarriers are that imaging agent integrates with chitosan polymer and provides monitoring of the treatment progress [28,29].

The optical properties, including photoluminescence (PL) spectra and UV-Vis absorption of CD-CS hybrid nanogels in the aqueous phase, were compared to CDs. Fig. 2 a compares the UV-vis spectrum of the CS nanogel, free CDs, and CCHNs. The UV-vis absorption spectra of the CDs exhibit an absorption peak at 280 nm, which is corresponded to the π-π* transition of the sp2 domains [31].

The CCHNs have an absorption peak at 260 nm compared to the CS nanogel, confirming the successful presence of CDs in CCHNs. The PL studies have shown that CCHNs suspension emitted blue light under UV-light (360 nm), while it was yellow color under visible light, Fig. 2b. The CCHNs were expected to show similar fluorescence properties to the free carbon dots, proving the successful embedding of CDs in the nanogel network. As shown in Fig. 2b, the PL emission wavelength of the CCHNs shifted to a longer wavelength (redshift). At the same time, the PL intensity declined gradually over an excitation wavelength of 340–440 nm, which might be caused by the surface energy traps of the fluorescence CDs embedded into the chitosan nanogel. The strongest fluorescence emission occurred at 450 nm with the excitation at λex = 360 nm. Using a quinine sulfate (QY: 54%) as a fluorescence standard, CCHNs and CDs quantum yield was measured to be 10% and 14%, respectively, under the excitation at 360 nm (Table 1). The relatively low quantum yield of CCHNs might be due to the interactions between chitosan and CDs, which would affect the emission properties of CDs by quenching the emitted photons. The results of functional groups and structural assessment of CS nanogel, CDs, and CCHNs were investigated through an FTIR spectrophotometer (Fig. 2c). In all the spectra, the characteristic peaks at 3000–3500 cm−1, 2850–3000 cm−1, and 1000–1350 cm−1 are likely attributed to OH/NH, CH stretching vibrations, and CN amine groups. In the spectrum of CS nanogel (Fig. 2c (a)), the significant peaks at 1642 and 1566 cm−1 are related to the imine CN bond (between the aldehyde residues of GA and amino groups of CS) and NH bending [32].

The sharp peaks of the CDs (Fig. 2c (b)) at 1700, 1649, and 1551 cm−1 were attributed to the CO carbonyl group, CO stretching vibration, and NH bending vibration. Indeed, the presence of functional groups on the surface of CDs is due to the hydrothermal carbonization [27]. The FTIR spectrum of CCHNs, presented in Fig. 2c (c), indicates a band at 1644 cm−1 corresponds to CO stretching and overlaps the imine CN band in the same region. The band at 1550 cm−1 is related to the NH bending vibration. The band at 1464 cm−1 shows the CH bending vibration of the alkyl group. As shown in Fig. 2c, the comparison of three spectra shows that the cross-linking process of CS nanogel and CDs was successfully acquired. Notably, the presence of a clear peak at 1644 cm−1 in the CCHNs curve, in addition to the CO stretching and NH bending of amide groups, confirms the interactions between chitosan and CDs.

The function of CCHNs, as a multifunctional nanocarrier, was evaluated with the anti-cancer drug (silibinin) as a model. The results demonstrated that the loading content and encapsulation efficiency percentages of Sil in the hybrid nanogels were 35% and 69%, respectively. The successful loading of hydrophobic drug into the CCHNs can be related to the presence of interior network structure in hybrid nanogel and the interactions between functional groups of CCHNs and Sil at pH = 7.4.

Fig. 3 displays the release rate of Sil from CCHNs in PBS solution (pH = 7.4 and 5) at 37 °C. The Sil-CCHNs displayed a 20% release at the first four hours, attributed to the drug attached to the nanogel surface. After this relatively fast release, a sustained release lasted up to 72 h. The amount of Sil-release from nanogel reached 52% under physiological conditions (pH = 7.4) after 72 h. In acidic conditions (pH = 5), the release rate increased to 70%.

The increased percentage of drug release from CCHNs in acidic pH might result from the pH sensitivity of hybrid nanogel. The amine and carboxyl groups of chitosan and carbon dots are protonated in an acidic medium liable for the intensified electrostatic repulsion between chitosan chains and carbon dots. Eventually, the network structure of chitosan degenerated, and the amount of drug release enhanced dramatically. This pH sensitivity of hybrid nanogel is appropriate for drug delivery, as the lysosomes and tumor microenvironments are acidic. Therefore, this acidic property can facilitate drug release from the nanogel [33].

To investigate the application of chitosan-carbon dot hybrid nanogels for bioimaging, the breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) was utilized to assess the cellular imaging based on the fluorescence properties of green CDs. Fig. 4 displays the fluorescent images of MCF-7 cells incubated with free CDs and CCHNs (obtained under the excitation of 377 nm by a fluorescence microscope). As can be observed from the microscope images, the CDs and nanogels possess the potential of being tracked in treated cells. In the first hour of incubation, cells exposed to the free CDs emitted strong fluorescent compared to cells with CDs embedded in CS nanogel because the fluorescence of CDs was quenched when CDs interacted with chitosan. However, the fluorescence intensity increased in the cells incubated with CCHNs after 4 h in the same condition. This can be explained probably for two reasons; (i) more CDs were introduced into the cells due to the interaction between the positive charge of nanogel and the negative charge of the mucosal surface, (ii) because of the low quenching efficiency due to the pH-sensitivity of hybrid nanogel behavior, and finally, the release of CDs from hybrid nanogel into the cells. Therefore, the fluorescent images of MCF-7 cells, treated by fluorescent nanocarrier, verify their cellular uptake ability and cell imaging [34].

To evaluate the biocompatibility of chitosan-CD hybrid nanogels and the therapeutic effects of loaded Sil in nanogel, an MTT assay was performed using the MCF-7 human breast cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 5 , more than 70% of the MCF-7 cells can survive upon 48 h treatment with CCHNs. On the other hand, drug-free CCHNs did not show significant toxicity against MCF-7 cells after 24 and 48 h, confirming the biocompatibility of the resultant CCHNs. To investigate the Sil effect on the cell viability, the Sil-CCHNs and free form of Sil have been used with the same drug concentrations. A relatively high increase in mortality of the MCF-7 cells was observed after 48 h of exposure to the Sil-CCHNs compared to the free form of Sil. The enhanced cytotoxicity of the Sil-CCHNs can be attributed to the increased entry of nanogels into the cell. An upsurge ensued in the cellular uptake of pH-responsive Sil-CCHNs in the drug release because of the acidic condition of cancer cells. Finally, these results demonstrated that the chitosan-CDs hybrid nanogels improve therapeutic efficacy as a multifunctional nanocarrier [27,30,34].

In this study, Sil-CCHN was prepared as a multifunctional nanocarrier to combine the fluorescent and therapeutic agents for cancer therapy. The chitosan nanocarrier provided network space for a high loading capacity, and the green-CDs, embedded in CCHNs, emitted bright fluorescence and has shown good stability. Considering the size of the nanoparticles based on TEM and FE-SEM analysis, they uptake into cancer cells and emit bright fluorescence under excitation wavelengths. Our study also demonstrated the efficacy of fluorescent nanogels in successfully loading the anti-cancer drug Sil and its release in response to the acidic condition in the MCF-7 cell. The MTT assay investigations confirmed that fluorescent nanocarriers are biocompatible and presented high therapeutic efficacy as Sil nanocarrier against MCF-7 breast cancer cells. Therefore, such a multifunctional nanocarrier has excellent potential for bioimaging, medical diagnostics, and therapeutic applications.
Financial support for this research was provided by Tabriz University of Medical Sciences with grant number of IR.TBZMED.VCR.REC.1397.114
The study was approved by the Committee of Clinical Research Ethics at Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Iran. (NO. 5/D/698435).
The authors confirm that the data supporting the findings of this work are available within the article.
All authors have read and approved for the subscription publication.

Fatemeh Hashemi: Investigation, Writing – original draft, Validation, Resources. Nasrin Mohajeri: Methodology, Writing – original draft, Conceptualization, Project administration. Fatemeh Radnia: Investigation, Methodology, Resources. Nosratollah Zarghami: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Validation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A special thanks to the Tabriz University of Medical Sciences for the usage of their facilities.
